Prie le  Secrétaire  général  de  continuer  à  améliorer  les  interventions internationales  faisant  suite  aux  catastrophes  naturelles,  de  lui  rendre  compte  à  ce sujet  à  sa  soixante  et  onzième  session  et  de  présenter  dans  son  rapport  des recommandations  sur  les  moyens  à  mettre  en  œuvre  pour  que  l'assistance humanitaire soit dispensée de manière à favoriser le passage de la phase des secours à celle de l'aide au développement.
items_en: 51. Calls upon Member States, the United Nations system and humanitarian organizations to provide emergency assistance in ways that contribute, as appropriate, to long-term recovery and development, including through the priority use of humanitarian capacity that enhances resilience and contributes to livelihoods, including but not limited to remittances, local food and service purchases, and social protection schemes;

52. Encourages the United Nations system and other humanitarian and development actors to provide support to humanitarian coordinators and resident coordinators to enhance their capacity, inter alia, to assist the Governments of the countries in which they are located in disaster preparedness and to coordinate similar activities of the country teams in support of national initiatives, and encourages the United Nations system and other relevant humanitarian actors to further strengthen their capacity to ensure the rapid and flexible deployment of humanitarian experts who can provide support to Governments and country teams immediately after a disaster;

53. Invites Member States, the United Nations and humanitarian and development organizations to identify ways to improve the current financial architecture in order to increase the coherence, predictability and flexibility of long-term risk management funding through multi-year strategies, including disaster preparedness, on the basis of a comprehensive risk assessment, so that resources are allocated as a matter of priority where the greatest risk exists;

54. Stresses the need to mobilize adequate, flexible and sustainable resources to finance recovery, preparedness and disaster risk reduction activities in order to ensure predictable and timely access to the resources required for humanitarian assistance in emergencies caused by natural disasters;

55. Welcomes the important achievements of the Central Emergency Response Fund, which has enabled a more timely and predictable response to humanitarian crises, stresses the importance of continuing to improve the functioning of the Fund, and in this regard urges the United Nations funds and programmes and the specialized agencies to review and evaluate, where necessary, their partnership policies and practices with a view to ensuring that the Fund promptly provides funds to implementing partners and that resources are used in the most efficient, effective, accountable and transparent manner possible;

56. Calls upon all Member States, and invites the private sector and all relevant individuals and institutions, to consider increasing their voluntary contributions to the Central Emergency Response Fund and to continue to strengthen the position of the Fund as the global reference agency for emergency response, and stresses that such contributions should be in addition to commitments already made to humanitarian programmes and should not be reduced from the resources allocated to international development cooperation;

57. Invites Member States, the private sector and all other relevant stakeholders to take advantage of their respective expertise, capacities and resources, and also to consider making voluntary contributions to humanitarian financing mechanisms;

58. Strongly encourages that disaster risk reduction and resilience-building should be seen as integral parts of sustainable development and should be given due attention in that context, as well as in the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development 4 and the Addis Ababa Programme of Action of the Third International Conference on Financing for Development 10, and that an approach be adopted that emphasizes the complementarity and coherence of those programmes and the Sendai Framework;

59. Urges all relevant actors to promote a comprehensive, coherent, systematic and people-centred risk management policy, including on the basis of the principles contained in the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, the Sendai Framework and the outcomes of the twenty-first session of the Conference of the Parties to the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change, the United Nations Conference on Housing and Sustainable Urban Development (Habitat III) and the World Summit on Humanitarian Action;

60. Welcomes the initiative taken by the Secretary-General in organizing the first World Summit on Humanitarian Action, held in Istanbul, Turkey, on 23 and 24 May 2016, notes the ongoing preparatory work and consultations, including regional and global consultations, requests the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs to ensure that Member States and stakeholders participate and contribute to this preparatory work, which should be conducted in a spirit of openness, dialogue and transparency, as well as in the preparation of the outcome document of the Summit, and requests the Secretary-General to continue to engage with Member States and other stakeholders and to present a plan of action with regard to the preparatory work for the Summit, including what is expected of Member States and stakeholders, as well as the expected results and scope and follow-up mechanisms envisaged;

61. Requests the Secretary-General to continue to improve international response to natural disasters and to report thereon to the General Assembly at its seventy-first session and to make recommendations in his report on ways and means to ensure that humanitarian assistance is provided in a manner that facilitates the transition from relief to development.

text_fr: 72 e séance plénière 10 décembre 2015
text_en: 72nd plenary meeting 10 December 2015
